source.title: ANF06. Fathers of the Third Century: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Dionysius the Great, Julius Africanus, Anatolius, and Minor Writers, Methodius, Arnobius

title: Chapter XLIII.

source.title: ANF06. Fathers of the Third Century: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Dionysius the Great, Julius Africanus, Anatolius, and Minor Writers, Methodius, Arnobius

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: I shall speak now with the utmost brevity of the veil of Moses and the ministration of death.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For I do not think that these things at least can introduce very much to the disparagement of the law.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: The text in question, then, proceeds thus: “But if the ministration of death, engraven in letters on the stones, was made in glory, so that the children of Israel could not stedfastly behold the face of Moses for the glory of his countenance; which glory was to be done away;” and so on.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Well, this passage at any rate acknowledges the existence of a glory on the countenance of Moses, and that surely is a fact favourable to our position.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And even although it is to be done away, and although there is a veil in the reading of the same, that does not annoy me or disturb me, provided there be glory in it still.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Neither is it the case, that whatever is to be done away is reduced thereby under all manner of circumstances to a condition of dishonour.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For when the Scripture speaks of glory, it shows us also that it had cognizance of differences in glory.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Thus it says: “There is one glory of the sun, and another glory of the moon, and another glory of the stars: for one star differeth from another star in glory.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Although, then, the sun has a greater glory than the moon, it does not follow that the moon is thereby reduced to a condition of dishonour.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And even thus, too, although my Lord Jesus Christ excelleth Moses in glory, as the lord excelleth the servant, it does not follow from this that the glory of Moses is to be scorned.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For in this way, too, we are able to satisfy our hearers, as the nature of the word itself carries the conviction with it in that we affirm what we allege on the authority of the Scriptures themselves, or verily make the proof of our statements all the clearer also by illustrations taken from them.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Thus, although a person kindles a lamp in the night-time, after the sun has once risen he has no further need of the paltry light of his lamp, on account of that effulgence of the sun which sends forth its rays all the world over; and yet, for all that, the man does not throw his lamp contemptuously away, as if it were something absolutely antagonistic to the sun; but rather, when he has once found out its use, he will keep it with all the greater carefulness.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Precisely in this way, then, the law of Moses served as a sort of guardian to the people, like the lamp, until the true Sun, who is our Saviour, should arise, even as the apostle also says to us: “And Christ shall give thee light.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: We must look, however, to what is said further on: “Their minds were blinded: for until this day remaineth the same veil in the reading of the Old Testament; it is untaken away, because it is done away in Christ.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For even unto this day, when Moses is read, the veil is upon their heart.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Nevertheless, when it shall turn to the Lord, the veil shall be taken away.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Now the Lord is that Spirit.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: What, then, is meant by this?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Is Moses present with us even unto this day?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Is it the case that he has never slept, that he has never gone to his rest, that he has never departed this life?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: How is it that this phrase “unto this day” is used here?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Well, only mark the veil, which is placed, where he says it is placed, on their hearts in their reading.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: This, therefore, is the word of censure upon the children of Israel, because they read Moses and yet do not understand him, and refuse to turn to the Lord; for it is He that was prophesied of by Moses as about to come.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: This, then, is the veil which was placed upon the face of Moses, and this also is his testament; for he says in the law: “A prince shall not be wanting from Judah, nor a leader from his thighs, until He come whose he is; and He will be the expectation of the nations: who shall bind His foal unto the vine, and His ass’s colt unto the choice vine; He shall wash His garments in wine, and His clothes in the blood of grapes; His eyes shall be suffused with wine, and His teeth white with milk;” and so on.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Moreover, he indicated who He was, and whence He was to come.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For he said: “The Lord God will raise up unto you a Prophet from among your brethren, like unto me: unto Him hearken ye.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Now it is plain that this cannot be understood to have been said of Jesus the son of Nun.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For there is nothing of this circumcision found in him.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: After him, too, there have still been kings from Judah; and consequently this prophecy is far from being applicable to him.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And this is the veil which is on Moses; for it was not, as some among the unlearned perhaps fancy, any piece of linen cloth, or any skin that covered his face.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But the apostle also takes care to make this plain to us, when he tells us that the veil is put on in the reading of the Old Testament, inasmuch as they who are called Israel from olden time still look for the coming of Christ, and perceive not that the princes have been wanting from Judah, and the leaders from his thighs; as even at present we see them in subjection to kings and princes, and paying tribute to these, without having any power left to them either of judgment or of punishment, such as Judah certainly had, for after he had condemned Thamar, he was able also to justify her.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: “But you will also see your life hang (in doubt) before your eyes.”
